articles.title: A Written Language for the Cherokee So Efficient It Was Thought to Be Magic
articles.summary: Sequoyah's invention revolutionized writing by creating a unique system that expressed specific sounds using symbols borrowed from Greek, Hebrew, and English, allowing Cherokee speakers to read and write Iroquoian language for the first time. This breakthrough had significant implications for Native American communities, leading to higher literacy rates among the Cherokee people compared to their non-Native counterparts. The widespread adoption of Sequoyah's syllabary also enabled the creation of a written constitution and other important documents, cementing its importance as a means of self-government and cultural memory.
